document.title: Zoo Storage and Indexing
document.purpose: Describe where Zoo project files are stored, how they are located via Machine-Root, and how indexing is handled.
author: Lion Kimbro
ChatGPT

overview.basic: Zoo stores projects as individual JSON files within a directory structure rooted at a Machine-Root entry.
overview.separation: This document describes storage and indexing only. The structure of individual project files is defined in zoo.project-json.format.v1.
overview.philosophy: Storage is simple and transparent. Indexes are optional and derived. The filesystem is the source of truth.

location.resolution: The root directory is obtained via Machine-Root using the key 'db-root-2026'.
location.note: The 'db-root-2026' key identifies a system-era root, not data limited to the year 2026.

project-files.structure: Each project is stored as a separate JSON file.
character: Each file represents exactly one project.
Files may be added, removed, or edited independently.
The filesystem layout is flat within the projects directory.

indexing.purpose: Provide a faster way to enumerate and search projects without scanning all project files.
character: The index is derived data and not authoritative.
The index may be rebuilt at any time from project files.
Systems should use the index for fast runtime enumeration and search.
On startup, applications may load the index into memory and operate from that in-memory copy.
Before writing amendments to the index, applications should reread the index from disk, apply the amendment to the fresh copy, and then write it back.
Systems must not rely on the index being present or up to date.

index-format.basic: The index contains a list of project summaries.

planned: GUI application for browsing, creating, and editing Zoo projects
CLI and Python API for programmatic access to Zoo
Tool for mapping filesystem folders to Zoo projects
Integration with Zookeep for repository-backed projects
tools.note: Tools operate on the filesystem representation and should not require a database layer.

future-considerations: Additional index files for specialized queries (e.g., by tag or date)
Linking systems to express relationships between projects
Background processes to maintain or validate indexes
conventions: Do not store filesystem paths inside project JSON files.
Treat the filesystem as the primary storage mechanism.
Treat index files as caches, not sources of truth.
Include enough summary information in the index to support fast browsing UI, including the hook field.
Prefer simplicity and inspectability over abstraction.
